Hey friends and welcome to Six Figure Emails number two. This is called the Anti-Launch Launch. It's one of my favorite email templates to send as an offer announcement or a launch date email. And we're actually going to open up with what not to do. So this is something that landed in my inbox and it is really the antithesis of what we're doing here. This is kind of a building curiosity. There's this green drapey thing for your eyes only. Today is the big day. We're only a few hours away from the huge Camtasia announcement. So this is what we want to avoid. We're going to talk about why we want to avoid this in just a second. But in case this is the first template you're watching, maybe you skipped an earlier one because you need to write a launch date email. This is how it goes down. This is how all of them go down. We're going to start off on background, on the template, talk about why we use it. These are the persuasion principles, where to use it within a launch or funnel, and then of course how to use it. So some powerful questions to ask to extract the content, then the template itself. So what that content is, the ordering of it, the sequencing of it for maximal effect. And then the next video right under this one, we'll take a look at an example to here down so you can see it all in action. Cool. Let's start with the background. As you may know, over 50% of your sales generally tend to come into last 48 hours. But why do we keep, or why do we accept that as the norm? That is the question really fueling a lot of this. There's so many stats and tendencies that we just take for granted without asking why. And this is my question. Why are more than half of qualified ideal perfectly targeted customers waiting until the very end to buy? And instead of depending solely on the urgency of a countdown timer to move product, why not take a stab at unpacking the psychology of what's happening here and actually try to front load some sales? Not decrease some sales that we were going to get in the final 48 hours, but add more sales to the beginning and have a more profitable launcher funnel in the first place. That's really what has been fueling this. And there is so much benefit to front loading more sales. The first thing is simply cash flow. If you have revenue coming in on day one of a launch, now you have more cash in hand to invest in paid traffic or retargeting for the people who visited your sales page and didn't buy. This is so key, especially during a live launch. Validation also for both you and your affiliate partners, if you have people mailing out for this and you have zero sales on day one, that gives them cold feet. That gets people a little more hesitant to be aggressive through the rest of your campaign or through their campaign and promoting your product. But if they can see that product is moving, people are buying, people are enrolling, that only builds more confidence to be even maybe more aggressive than they intended to be. So this is massive. This is why you really want to front load more sales. Social proof. You can either buzz people on social posting about joining it, more people chiming in to answer questions on social media when people are asking about the program. You can have case studies that you could highlight later in your sequence. You could add surveys on the thank you page. That's something we've done successfully as well. And be able to use that survey data in your closing emails. Have people in your Facebook group. All these things are possible when you have people who enroll or join your program or buy a product earlier on in the funnel rather than later. Then the final one is that you're really just setting the expectation. Even if they don't buy from this email, you've at least set the expectation that maybe they should have. You've really started to raise the stakes early on, which primes them for that closing sequence. Most people won't buy off of one email. They'll buy from a combination of emails. And if you could really set the tone in the beginning, even if they don't buy, you may have already overcome many hurdles to the eventual sale. So that's really more reasons than you should need for really leading with your best stuff at the beginning of that campaign. And we're going to show you exactly how to do that. So first, why do people wait? What is really going on? Why people wait until the final buzzer to buy in the launch? Well, here's what's going on. We make it all about us and our product creation journey. We talk so much about putting the focus on the consumer or the end customer. But when it comes to launching products for whatever reason, we don't give them a reason to buy right away. We just talk about how we're releasing this big shiny thing and how we've been refining it for years and months and it's our life's work and blah, blah, blah. And like people just don't care. So that is one of the reasons they wait because we're not making it about them and the issues they're trying to overcome at all. We're just really making it about us and we're making such a big deal out of it. So here's the thing. We don't want them to take a massive leap into our context. We want to have permission to step into theirs. There's a very big difference. One of them is much easier to do than the other. We're asking them to abandon the current context they were in and very comfortable in and jump into our world. That's scary. But if we are simply saying, this is your world as we perceive it, can we step in it with our product that lowers resistance a whole lot more. Last thing of course is we make it too big of a deal instead of a natural process that syncs up with their existing journey. Once again, that creates unnecessary resistance and has people hold off until the very end where the artificial urgency or the urgency of a timer, mind you, kicks in and makes that decision for them. And finally, our mammalian brains are so not down with this huge, mega massive deal kind of thing. Think about major life decisions like buying a house or getting married or bringing home a puppy, even a cute pug with a really awesome beanie. All these things required time. They invited procrastination, maybe a lot of resistance and stress and arguments with their spouses. And that's because we want to be safe. We don't want to take these massive decisions. And when we talk about our launch being a really big deal as we've been trained to do and a lot of people still do, it just triggers all these defenses and fears about engaging in it. So, most of all, beyond anything, when we don't lead with a sales email at the beginning that really paints the picture of what is going on in their world, not our world, we tend to trigger the objection that I can wait. This is one thing that most first day offer emails or launch day emails don't do because the launch sequence is probably going to go on for another five days, seven days, even 10 days in some cases. This is the number one thing that readers are thinking. They're like, this just launched now, I have at least seven days to think about this. I could wait until the later emails to jump on it. So rarely, if ever, does a launch day email actually start addressing the objection of I can wait till later. So this is what we are going to be focusing on doing. What we do is we build up all this anticipation and energy in our pre-launch, whether you're doing a pre-launch video series, a webinar, or even some pre-launch emails starting to seed your actual offer. So we build up all that desire, all that desire to solve a certain problem, and then we launch it. But we launch it as a big deal to us and we don't give them a reason to buy now. And essentially what we're doing is we're letting them off the hook. This is one of my favorite football moments of all time. So bonus points for anyone who gets that reference. So here's what happens. Of course we sprinkle in the urgency. We could talk about limited number of spots, disappearing fast action bonuses, countdown timers, although I don't recommend countdown timers unless it's 48 hours or less to the close. So we do have all these tools and we do use them. But we miss the opportunity to leverage the most powerful and natural triggers possible. So we have the consistency principle. We can call out the procrastination behavior and hold them to a higher standard. They opted into this launch funnel. If they've been participating in the webinar, if they've been doing other pre-launch activities, there is something that we could call them to be consistent with. This might be the time where we take a stand for them and be like, you've already said that you have these prompts and this is something that you would like to experience or this is a solution you'd like to experience now is the time to do it. The other thing, this is really the biggest tool we have in our toolkit is highlighting the real risk in not acting today. So not the risk that they might forget to buy our thing or there's only 72 hours left or whatever it is, but the real actual risk that they encounter in their life. So this is about reminding them that the house is indeed burning and they need to get out right now. So you could call the fire department in a few days, but your house will already be burned to ashes. What we want them to say is you called us to come over. You joined this funnel voluntarily because you are experiencing these problems in your lives. And yes, you can wait another week or two to get on board, but why would you do that when you're experiencing these problems right now and there could be a cost associated with even waiting? So really the important point you want to drive home here is Rome is already burning. Just because it launched and there's another week until CART closes, they're experiencing these problems right now. There's an effect and implications to letting that problem go unresolved even for another few days. So you want to create that natural urgency by reminding them that Rome is burning. And the end goal of this is that you must get your prospect intimately aware of the fact that the cost of waiting, even for a few days, is far greater than the minor anxiety of completing the transaction today. This is the main goal of the first email of any launch campaign. Even if they don't buy from this, at least they have attuned to this fact and the stakes of waiting. Cool. So as a whole, this is what this email is coaching. We want to coach progress or sustained momentum. This is especially true if this launch email, this launch day emails within the context of a pre-launch where they've already been participating in either a webinar or a video series or a challenge or whatever that pre-launch look like, you want to sustain momentum. And that's what this email does is it's just continuing that journey, not just having them do all these things like a challenge and a video series and then let them off the hook and chill. So we want to sustain that. We want to coach the fact that there is a burning fire and then need to extinguish it right away. So this is it. We don't say our product is out. It's a big deal. You should buy it. We say, you know, yeah, at launch, but this fire in your life has been burning for a while. And I think it's time that we actually fix it right now. We want the focus to be on that, not just the price of our offer and the resistance that comes with suddenly having a product inserted into the conversation. All right. We also want to talk about consistency and gains the behavior of the procrastinator. So we're going to call that out right from the beginning that a lot of people might want to procrastinate because it's the first day of the offer. We're going to call that out and call them to a higher action. Then we'll talk about how and why they'll be in a more advantageous position to benefit than those who sign up or buy later in a very real non-artificial way. Why, what makes them actually in a better place to buy now than later? I'm sure if you thought about your product right now, you could think of a few reasons. And then the last one is naturalness. So this is where we take a small step into their context with our product instead of asking them to take a giant leap into ours. This is the mindset that we're entering this email with, and it will totally shift the way you actually go about writing this email and avoid having the first one we opened up with, which is a huge deal, come into a world, see what we've been working on. That's not what we want to be doing. We want to be stepping into theirs with our product and putting out the fire. Cool. All right. Before I lose my breath, one final thought. Your product isn't the big deal. Just drill that home. Your product isn't the big deal. The fact that they can now put out the fire that's been burning in their life. This entire time is let that be your focus. And it's very hard to not write an effective launch day email. So where to use it? Self-explanatory. This is really a launch day announcement for a live launch or one of the very first sales emails or offer announcement. If you're running this in a nevergreen campaign, how to use it. So here are some questions to ask that can really drive the content you use here. So what microactions have they already taken that you can hold them consistent to? So that could have been downloading a lead magnet that solves part of the problem or puts a part of the fire could have been joining you for a webinar. Anything that got them in that funnel to begin with is probably what you want to be looking at here. In what ways is Rome burning for them? How has it been burning for long before you came along? So once again, this isn't about your launch. You are just a firefighter who's been called. They asked you to come and put it out. So how has Rome been burning for them in very specific ways? How will your prospect be in a worse position if they buy in a few days? Well, will that fire have already consumed between now and then? What benefits are they leaving on the table? How have they been trying and struggling to put out the fire on their own? How is a product launch disingenuous to you? Now, this is a really good one. Even though your product is launching, what elements have you been developing and testing and proving for months or years? Just take the whole launch idea out of it. What makes this product actually years in development? And what results or case studies can you point to? So these are questions you want to explore and journal on before writing the actual email when you are ready to write the email. The template goes like this. You're going to come out and I'll set the offers now live without making a mega deal with it. You could add some social proof or benefit that people who are already using it are experiencing it, but here's really the crux of it. You want to bring into why it's not an actual launch, you know, it's not a launch for them because Rome has been burning and talk about what that looks like for them, um, describe the exact context and problem of Rome being burned down as specifically as you can and remind them that the fire has been burning long before you got there. It's not like you created this problem. You're just here to put it out. Um, and then you'll talk about why it's not a launch for you. Yes, the product is being launched, but you already have credibility proof case studies, um, related to the techniques, um, or related to the attributes of the product. Um, then call out a negative trait like the procrastination, you know, this is really the number one thing you want to fight against on your launch. People will be attuned to the fact that they probably have a few days to take an action. You don't want them to chill out on that. You want to call a procrastination, tell them, um, you know, tell them, you know, how waiting might actually cost them or, you know, what else might be consumed by that fire and just hold them consistent to a higher standard. And then you'll have a call to action, which just shows naturalness. Like this is a natural next step to what's already happened. Rome is already burning. They called the fire department. You're here. Let's do this. You want me to put it out? So that's what a natural next step looks like. The cost of continuing to wait. Another call to action. And you can use the PS to disarm one or two objections. All right. So that is it for the anti launch launch. We're going to take a breather and then you can meet me in the next video where we break down an example that we used here at copy hackers. I'll see you in the next video.
